title: Using Logic to Investigate Homeomorphism Groups of Manifolds
abstract: It is a classical fact that countable groups of homeomorphisms of the interval and the circle are characterized by purely algebraic properties, namely linear and circular orderability. It remains a difficult and deep problem to understand countable groups that can act by homeomorphisms on compact manifolds in dimension two or more. I will discuss some new ways of investigating groups acting on manifolds through ideas from mathematical logic and descriptive set theory. This talk will include work that is joint with Sang-hyun Kim, J. De la Nuez Gonzalez, and Yash Lodha.
tags: Joint IAS/PU Groups and Dynamics Seminar

proof_stages.label: Reconstruction and subgroup questions for compact manifolds
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a compact topological manifold M, the groups Homeo(M) and Homeo_0(M), and frames questions about recovering M, understanding possible subgroups, and measuring subgroup complexity.

proof_stages.label: Classical abstract rigidity of homeomorphism groups
proof_stages.summary: Whittaker-type rigidity says that an abstract isomorphism between identity components of homeomorphism groups of compact manifolds is induced by conjugation by a homeomorphism, with analogous smooth results forcing the differentiability class.

proof_stages.label: First-order theories as syntactic rigidity
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces first-order group theory and elementary equivalence, then states a syntactic version of Whittaker rigidity: a single first-order sentence can distinguish the homeomorphism group of any compact connected manifold among such groups.

proof_stages.label: Theory complexity and set-theoretic instability
proof_stages.summary: By Gödel-coding a first-order theory as a subset of the natural numbers, complexity becomes computability-theoretic, yielding ZFC-undecidability for full homeomorphism-group theories and independence phenomena for noncompact manifolds under V=L versus projective determinacy.

proof_stages.label: Rubin's local-action reconstruction theorem
proof_stages.summary: Rubin's framework defines rigid stabilizers and local moving, local dense, and local approximating actions, and proves that a locally dense group action reconstructs the underlying locally compact space equivariantly from group theory.

proof_stages.label: First-order Rubin theorem for locally approximating subgroups
proof_stages.summary: For a triangulable closed M, if a locally approximating subgroup G of Homeo(M) is elementarily equivalent to a locally approximating subgroup H of Homeo(N), then M and N are homotopy equivalent, with homeomorphism consequences in low dimensions and Borel-conjecture cases.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic interpretation and computability lower bounds
proof_stages.summary: Using Thompson's group T as a guide, the speaker states that every locally approximating homeomorphism group interprets arithmetic, forcing its first-order theory to have at least the complexity of full arithmetic and allowing higher Turing-jump complexities.

examples.name: Nonabelian free groups with the same theory

examples.name: Thompson's group T on the circle
examples.note: The dyadic piecewise-linear circle group is presented as the guiding case whose bi-interpretability with arithmetic motivates the general theorem for locally approximating groups.
